questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a barrier in parallel computing? Give an example of when it is needed. (2 marks)
keyPoints: A barrier ensures all participating threads reach a specific synchronization point before any of them proceed further.
Example: In a multi-phase computation (e.g., matrix computation), all threads must complete Phase 1 before any thread starts Phase 2, because Phase 2 depends on Phase 1 results.

questions.question: Explain the fundamental difference between a mutex and a semaphore in coordinating access to shared resources. (3 marks)
keyPoints: A mutex (mutual exclusion) ensures only one thread can access a shared resource at a time (binary lock). It has ownership -- the thread that locks must unlock.
A semaphore controls access by maintaining a count of available permits, allowing multiple threads to access a pool of N identical resources. P/V operations can be performed by different threads.
Mutex is for exclusive access to one resource; semaphore is for controlling access to a pool of resources.

questions.question: Describe how a condition variable is used to coordinate threads, including the roles of wait, signal, and broadcast operations. (5 marks)
keyPoints: A condition variable allows a thread to wait until a particular condition based on shared state becomes true.
Wait operation: atomically releases the associated mutex and blocks the thread until it is signaled. This avoids busy-waiting.
Signal operation: wakes up one waiting thread. The woken thread re-acquires the mutex before continuing.
Broadcast operation: wakes up all waiting threads. Each woken thread re-acquires the mutex and re-checks the condition (spurious wakeup protection).
Always used in conjunction with a mutex: the mutex protects the shared state that the condition depends on.

questions.question: What are the four Coffman conditions for deadlock? Explain each and describe one strategy to prevent deadlock by breaking one of these conditions. (8 marks)
keyPoints: Mutual Exclusion: At least one resource must be held in a non-sharable mode.
Hold and Wait: A process holding resources can request additional resources.
No Preemption: Resources cannot be forcibly taken from a process.
Circular Wait: A circular chain of processes, each waiting for a resource held by the next.
All four must hold simultaneously for deadlock.
Prevention example: Break circular wait by imposing a total ordering on resources. All processes must request resources in ascending order. This eliminates cycles.
Other strategies: require all resources at once (breaks hold-and-wait), allow preemption (breaks no-preemption).
